_meta.purpose: Canonical, machine-readable source of truth for every coaching claim in Yalla. The app reads this (tips, the browsable Library, DOI links); EVIDENCE.md is the human-readable companion kept in step with it.

schema.categories: ordered list for the in-app Library; each {id,label,blurb}
schema.evidenceLevels: legend for the `evidence` tag on each study (causal strength)
schema.sourceTiers: legend for the `tier` tag on each study (source quality). We prioritise meta-analyses/reviews in reputable journals; single small cohorts are flagged.
schema.studies: keyed map of source records {authors,year,title,journal,design,n,doi,url,evidence,tier,finding,caveat}. `finding` is the concrete, verified result.
schema.advice: array of in-app advice items {id,study,category,audience,tone,strength,text,detail}. `audience` = ['all'] or goal/context list. `tone` = 'do' (imperative ok) | 'linked' (associational only). `strength` = 'standard' (default) | 'weak' (single small cohort — show sparingly / Library-only).

rules.sourcePriority: Prefer meta-analyses, systematic reviews and umbrella reviews in reputable journals (BJSM, Lancet, JAMA, BMJ, PNAS, Sports Medicine, etc.) over single small cohorts. Tag each study `tier`; flag weak ones.
rules.tone: tone='do' only when the study is causal/causal-leaning. tone='linked' (correlational/marker) must use 'linked to / tends to' and must NOT promise lifespan.
rules.doi: All DOIs verified to resolve to the cited paper (2026-06).

categories.label: Training
categories.blurb: How to actually drive muscle and strength: volume, frequency, progressive overload.

categories.label: Recovery & sleep
categories.blurb: Rest, maintenance on a busy week, the post-session window, sleep, and what soreness does (and doesn't) mean.

categories.label: Nutrition
categories.blurb: Protein, energy balance for gaining or cutting, fibre, hydration, coffee.

categories.label: Move for life
categories.blurb: How everyday movement and fitness track with living longer and healthier.

categories.label: Body signals
categories.blurb: Simple self-tests that mirror your overall health (predictors, not levers).

categories.label: Mind & happiness
categories.blurb: Mood, outlook, relationships, kindness and habits linked to a happier, longer life.

categories.label: Injury care & prevention
categories.blurb: Preventing common niggles and self-managing them if they flare. General guidance, not medical advice — see a clinician for significant or lasting pain.

evidenceLevels.causal: RCT, or meta-analysis of RCTs. Randomisation rules out most confounding — we can say 'do X → get Y'.
evidenceLevels.causal-leaning: Controlled experiments / mechanistic work / guideline synthesis, but not a clean RCT for the exact claim. Direction sound, magnitude less certain.
evidenceLevels.correlational: Observational (cohort / meta-analysis of cohorts). Adjusted, but residual confounding + reverse causation remain. Association only.
evidenceLevels.correlational+mech: Observational for the headline outcome, but acute RCTs / mechanism explain why. Plausibly causal, not proven.
evidenceLevels.marker: Observational, and the measured thing is a proxy for health, not a lever you can train into longevity. Predicts, doesn't promise.

sourceTiers.meta: Meta-analysis, systematic review or umbrella review (strongest; preferred).
sourceTiers.review: Narrative review, clinical guideline or expert consensus over the evidence.
sourceTiers.cohort: Large prospective/retrospective cohort or meta-analysis of cohorts.
sourceTiers.small-cohort: Single small or narrow cohort — weakest; flag and deprioritise.

sch17.authors: Schoenfeld BJ, Ogborn D, Krieger JW
sch17.title: Dose-response relationship between weekly resistance training volume and increases in muscle mass: a systematic review and meta-analysis
sch17.finding: Graded dose–response: each added weekly set ≈ +0.37% muscle gain; near-maximal benefit around ~10+ sets/muscle/week.

drr.authors: Pelland JC, Remmert JF, Robinson ZP, Hinson SR, Zourdos MC
drr.title: The resistance training dose-response: meta-regressions exploring the effects of weekly volume and frequency on muscle hypertrophy and strength gain
drr.note: Counting indirect (secondary) muscle sets as 0.5 best explained hypertrophy, which is how this app weighs assistance work.
drr.finding: More weekly sets → more growth across the studied range; counting indirect/secondary sets as 0.5 best fit the hypertrophy data; higher frequency helps accommodate volume.

sch16.authors: Schoenfeld BJ, Ogborn D, Krieger JW
sch16.title: Effects of resistance training frequency on measures of muscle hypertrophy: a systematic review and meta-analysis
sch16.finding: At matched weekly volume, training a muscle ~2×/week modestly out-grew once weekly.

sch10.title: The mechanisms of muscle hypertrophy and their application to resistance training
sch10.note: Progressive tension overload is a primary driver of growth — why “growing” needs a rising trend, not just an adequate dose held flat.
sch10.finding: Mechanical tension from progressively heavier/effortful loading is a primary hypertrophy driver (metabolic stress and muscle damage secondary).

plotkin.authors: Plotkin D, Coleman M, Van Every D, et al.
plotkin.title: Progressive overload without progressing load? The effects of load or repetition progression on muscular adaptations
plotkin.note: Overload via added reps or added load drove similar growth — why the trend tracks total volume (load×reps×sets), not load alone.
plotkin.finding: Progressing reps and progressing load produced near-identical hypertrophy and strength.

rpvol.title: Training volume landmarks for muscle growth (MEV/MAV/MRV)
rpvol.note: Basis for the ~10-set weekly target ring.
rpvol.finding: A productive weekly-volume window exists (~MEV 10 → MAV 20 sets/muscle); too little under-stimulates, too much adds fatigue; planned deloads clear accumulated fatigue.

mps.authors: Phillips SM, Tipton KD, Aarsland A, Wolf SE, Wolfe RR
mps.title: Mixed muscle protein synthesis and breakdown after resistance exercise in humans
mps.design: Experimental, tracer-measured FSR at rest/3/24/48 h
mps.note: Muscle-building signalling is elevated ~24–48 h per session, the basis for tracking volume by the week.
mps.finding: Muscle protein synthesis rose sharply after a session — roughly doubling (~+100%) by 24 h — and stayed elevated for about 24–48 h.
mps.caveat: Untrained subjects; window is shorter in trained lifters.

bickel.authors: Bickel CS, Cross JM, Bamman MM
bickel.title: Exercise dosing to retain resistance-training adaptations in young and older adults
bickel.design: Controlled trial: 16-wk build, then 32-wk reduced dose
bickel.note: After a build phase, muscle size was maintained on as little as ~1/3 (older) to ~1/9 (young) of the volume that built it — the basis for the maintenance-volume floor below which a muscle reads as “holding”, not growing.
bickel.finding: After building, size/strength were maintained on as little as ~1/3 of the volume in older adults and ~1/9 in young adults (down to ~1 set, 1×/week) over 32 weeks.

mujika.title: Detraining: loss of training-induced physiological and performance adaptations. Part I — short-term insufficient training stimulus
mujika.note: With too little stimulus, muscle fibres atrophy and strength declines within weeks — the basis for the “shrinking” state when weekly volume drops below maintenance.
mujika.finding: Strength is largely retained for ~2–4 weeks without training; longer cessation brings measurable fibre atrophy and aerobic decline.

vigotsky.authors: Vigotsky AD, Halperin I, Trajano GS, Vieira TM
vigotsky.title: Longing for a longitudinal proxy: acutely measured surface EMG amplitude is not a validated predictor of muscle hypertrophy
vigotsky.note: Why this app weighs whole sets, not per-exercise EMG “activation”.
vigotsky.finding: Acute surface-EMG 'activation' is not a validated predictor of long-term muscle growth.

morton.authors: Morton RW, Murphy KT, McKellar SR, et al.
morton.title: A systematic review, meta-analysis and meta-regression of the effect of protein supplementation on resistance training-induced gains in muscle mass and strength in healthy adults
morton.finding: Protein supplementation added ~+0.3 kg lean mass plus strength; benefit plateaued at about 1.6 g/kg/day.

slater.authors: Slater GJ, Dieter BP, Marsh DJ, Helms ER, Shaw G, Iraki J
slater.title: Is an energy surplus required to maximize skeletal muscle hypertrophy associated with resistance training?
slater.finding: A modest energy surplus supports muscle gain; large surpluses add mostly fat, especially in trained lifters.

garthe.authors: Garthe I, Raastad T, Refsnes PE, Sundgot-Borgen J
garthe.title: Effect of nutritional intervention on body composition and performance in elite athletes
garthe.finding: Faster weight gain (+3.9% vs +1.5% body weight) added more fat without more lean mass — a slower, controlled surplus keeps gains leaner.

helms.authors: Helms ER, Aragon AA, Fitschen PJ
helms.title: Evidence-based recommendations for natural bodybuilding contest preparation: nutrition and supplementation
helms.finding: For lean fat loss: a deficit giving ~0.5–1%/week loss, protein ~2.3–3.1 g/kg lean mass, fat 15–30% of calories, while continuing to lift.

paluch.title: Daily steps and all-cause mortality: meta-analysis of 15 cohorts
paluch.design: Meta-analysis of prospective cohorts, median 7.1 y
paluch.finding: Mortality fell with more steps, plateauing ~6,000–8,000/day at age ≥60 and ~8,000–10,000 under 60; top vs bottom quartile ~40–53% lower (HR 0.47–0.60). The 10,000 figure was a 1960s marketing slogan.

odonovan.authors: O'Donovan G, Lee I-M, Hamer M, Stamatakis E
odonovan.title: 'Weekend warrior' and other activity patterns and mortality
odonovan.finding: 'Weekend warriors' (1–2 sessions/wk meeting weekly totals) had ~30% lower all-cause mortality vs inactive (HR 0.70) — similar to regularly active people (HR 0.65).
odonovan.caveat: Self-reported activity; the unwell exercise less.

stamatakis.title: Wearable-measured vigorous intermittent lifestyle physical activity (VILPA) and mortality
stamatakis.finding: A median ~4.4 min/day of brief vigorous bursts was linked to 26–30% lower all-cause/cancer and 32–34% lower CVD mortality; ~3 one-minute bouts/day ≈ HR 0.61 all-cause.

momma.title: Muscle-strengthening activities and mortality / non-communicable disease
momma.finding: About 30–60 min/week of muscle-strengthening was linked to 10–17% lower all-cause, CVD, cancer and diabetes mortality — J-shaped, with no extra mortality benefit beyond ~1 h/wk.
momma.caveat: Mortality endpoint observational (the training→strength link itself is RCT-backed).

mandsager.title: Cardiorespiratory fitness and long-term mortality (treadmill testing)
mandsager.finding: Higher cardiorespiratory fitness tracked with lower mortality and showed no upper limit; elite vs low fitness HR 0.20 (low fitness ~5× the risk). Being unfit rivalled or exceeded smoking as a risk factor.

diaz.title: Patterns of sedentary behavior and mortality
diaz.finding: Both greater total sitting and longer uninterrupted bouts (≥60–90 min) independently tracked with higher mortality, dose-dependently; sitting accrued in short bouts (<30 min) fared best.
diaz.caveat: Acute RCTs support the mechanism (movement breaks steady post-meal glucose).

leong.title: Prognostic value of grip strength (PURE study)
leong.finding: Each 5 kg lower grip strength was linked to ~16% higher all-cause and ~17% higher CV mortality — outperforming systolic blood pressure as a predictor.
leong.caveat: Grip is a proxy for whole-body strength/health, not a lever — training grip alone won't add years.

brito.title: Ability to sit and rise from the floor as a predictor of mortality
brito.finding: Lowest sitting-rising scorers (0–3 of 10) had roughly 5–6× the all-cause mortality of top scorers (8–10).
brito.caveat: Single small cohort; a proxy for strength/balance/flexibility, not a lever. Below our meta-analysis-first bar — shown sparingly.

laukkanen.authors: Laukkanen T, Khan H, Zaccardi F, Laukkanen JA
laukkanen.title: Sauna bathing and fatal cardiovascular and all-cause mortality
laukkanen.finding: 4–7 sauna sessions/week vs once: all-cause mortality HR 0.60, fatal CVD HR 0.50, sudden cardiac death HR 0.37.
laukkanen.caveat: Single-sex, single-population (Finnish men). Below our meta-analysis-first bar — shown sparingly.

dmitrieva.title: High-normal serum sodium, biological aging, chronic disease, mortality
dmitrieva.finding: Serum sodium >142 mmol/L (a marker of lower hydration) was linked to ~39% higher chronic-disease risk and ~50% higher odds of being 'biologically older'; >144 mmol/L to ~21% higher premature-mortality risk.
dmitrieva.caveat: Sodium is an indirect hydration proxy; causation unproven.

reynolds.authors: Reynolds A, Mann J, Cummings J, Winter N, Mete E, Te Morenga L
reynolds.title: Carbohydrate quality and human health: systematic reviews & meta-analyses
reynolds.finding: Each +8 g/day of fibre ≈ 7% lower all-cause mortality (RR 0.93); highest vs lowest intake 15–30% lower all-cause/CV mortality. Target ~25–29 g/day. RCTs confirm fibre improves weight, blood pressure and cholesterol.
reynolds.caveat: Mortality link observational; risk-factor improvements are RCT-grade.

poole.authors: Poole R, Kennedy OJ, Roderick P, et al.
poole.title: Coffee consumption and health: umbrella review
poole.finding: 3–4 cups/day vs none was linked to ~17% lower all-cause mortality (RR 0.83), the largest benefit at ~3 cups; overall 'more likely to benefit than harm'.

windred.title: Sleep regularity a stronger predictor of mortality than sleep duration
windred.finding: The most regular sleepers had 20–48% lower all-cause mortality; sleep regularity out-predicted sleep duration.

holtlunstad.authors: Holt-Lunstad J, Smith TB, Layton JB
holtlunstad.title: Social relationships and mortality risk: a meta-analysis
holtlunstad.finding: Stronger social ties were linked to ~50% higher odds of survival (OR 1.50) — an effect comparable to quitting smoking and larger than obesity or inactivity.

lee.title: Optimism and exceptional longevity (2 cohorts)
lee.design: Prospective cohorts (Nurses' Health Study; VA Normative Aging)
lee.finding: The most optimistic had 11–15% longer lifespans and 50–70% greater odds of reaching 85, after adjusting for health behaviours.

alimujiang.title: Life purpose and mortality, US adults >50
alimujiang.finding: Adults with the lowest sense of life purpose had ~2.4× the all-cause mortality of those with the highest (HR 2.43), independent of wealth and health.

bavishi.authors: Bavishi A, Slade MD, Levy BR
bavishi.title: A chapter a day: book reading and longevity
bavishi.finding: Reading books more than 3.5 h/week was linked to 23% lower mortality (HR 0.80; ~23-month survival advantage) — books specifically, not newspapers or magazines.
bavishi.caveat: Observational; book readers differ in many ways.

singh.authors: Singh B, Olds T, Curtis R, et al.
singh.title: Effectiveness of physical activity for depression, anxiety and distress: umbrella review
singh.design: Umbrella review of meta-analyses of RCTs
singh.finding: Physical activity had medium effects vs usual care on depression (−0.43), anxiety (−0.42) and distress (−0.60) — comparable to or greater than therapy/medication; higher intensity helped more.

white.authors: White MP, Alcock I, Grellier J, et al.
white.title: Spending at least 120 minutes a week in nature is associated with good health and wellbeing
white.finding: People getting ≥120 min/week in nature were markedly more likely to report good health and high wellbeing; no benefit below 120 min, and it didn't matter if achieved in one visit or several.

carr.authors: Carr A, Cullen K, Keeney C, et al.
carr.title: Effectiveness of positive psychology interventions: systematic review & meta-analysis
carr.finding: Positive-psychology practices (gratitude, savouring, using strengths, kindness) had small-to-medium effects: wellbeing g=0.39, depression g=−0.39, anxiety g=−0.62, sustained at 3-month follow-up.

curry.authors: Curry OS, Rowland LA, Van Lissa CJ, et al.
curry.title: Happy to help? Acts of kindness and the well-being of the actor: meta-analysis
curry.finding: Performing acts of kindness reliably boosted the giver's own wellbeing — a small-to-medium effect (d=0.28), regardless of who the kindness was aimed at.

lauersen.authors: Lauersen JB, Bertelsen DM, Andersen LB
lauersen.title: Effectiveness of exercise interventions to prevent sports injuries: meta-analysis of RCTs
lauersen.finding: Strength training cut overall sports injuries to under a third (RR ~0.32) and overuse injuries roughly in half (RR 0.53); proprioception/balance training also helped; stretching did not.

vandyk.authors: van Dyk N, Behan FP, Whiteley R
vandyk.title: Nordic hamstring exercise halves the rate of hamstring injuries: meta-analysis
vandyk.finding: Programmes including the Nordic hamstring exercise roughly halved hamstring-injury rates (RR 0.49, ~51% reduction).

injAnkle.title: Diagnosis, treatment and prevention of ankle sprains: update of an evidence-based clinical guideline
injAnkle.finding: Functional support plus early controlled weight-bearing beats immobilisation; neuromuscular/balance training roughly halves (~50%) the risk of re-spraining.

injKnee.title: 2018 Consensus statement on exercise therapy and physical interventions to treat patellofemoral pain
injKnee.finding: Combined hip- and knee-focused exercise therapy with load management improves patellofemoral (kneecap) pain and function more than rest or passive care.

injBack.title: Prevention and treatment of low back pain: evidence, challenges, and promising directions
injBack.finding: For non-specific low back pain, guidelines converge on reassurance, staying active and avoiding bed rest; most episodes settle, and exercise helps recovery and prevention.

injShoulder.title: Progressive exercise compared with best-practice advice, with or without corticosteroid injection, for rotator cuff disorders (GRASP): a randomised controlled trial
injShoulder.finding: A single best-practice advice + home-exercise session matched a longer physio-led progressive programme; adding a corticosteroid injection gave no meaningful extra benefit.

injElbow.authors: Coombes BK, Bisset L, Vicenzino B
injElbow.title: Management of lateral elbow tendinopathy: one size does not fit all
injElbow.design: Clinical synthesis; plus Coombes 2013 RCT (JAMA, n=165)
injElbow.finding: Active loading/grip management beats rest for tennis elbow; corticosteroid injection worsens long-term recurrence — 72% recurred by 1 year vs 8% with physiotherapy (Coombes 2013 RCT, JAMA, doi:10.1001/jama.2013.129).

advice.text: Aim for roughly 10+ hard sets per muscle each week — that's where growth reliably kicks in.
advice.detail: Graded dose–response, each set ≈ +0.37% gain, near-max ~10 sets/wk (Schoenfeld 2017, meta-analysis).

advice.text: Under about 10 weekly sets per muscle you're likely leaving growth on the table — but you don't need to live in the gym to clear that bar.
advice.detail: Benefit accrues toward ~10 sets/muscle/wk (Schoenfeld 2017).

advice.text: Hit each muscle about twice a week — the same sets split over two days tend to grow a little more than one big session.
advice.detail: ~2×/wk modestly beat 1×/wk at matched volume (Schoenfeld 2016, meta-analysis).

advice.text: Got a lagging muscle? Add a second short session for it during the week rather than piling every set onto one day.
advice.detail: Higher frequency at equal volume favoured growth (Schoenfeld 2016).

advice.text: Muscle grows when you ask a bit more of it over time — add a rep or a little weight once a set starts to feel easy.
advice.detail: Mechanical tension via progressive overload is a primary growth driver (Schoenfeld 2010).

advice.text: Challenging loads lifted with real effort create the mechanical tension that tells a muscle to grow — that's the core of it.
advice.detail: Tension is the principal hypertrophy mechanism (Schoenfeld 2010).

advice.text: Can't add weight today? Add a rep. Progressing reps builds muscle just as well as adding load.
advice.detail: Rep- vs load-progression gave near-identical growth over 8 wk (Plotkin 2022, RCT, n=43).

advice.text: More weekly sets generally means more growth — but assistance work counts only about half, so train each muscle directly too.
advice.detail: Secondary sets weighted 0.5 best fit hypertrophy data (Pelland 2024, meta-regression).

advice.text: Once you're past ~10 sets for a muscle, spreading them over more days lets you add volume without each session dragging.
advice.detail: Frequency helps accommodate higher volume (Pelland 2024).

advice.text: There's a productive range for volume: too little won't grow, too much just piles on fatigue. About 10–20 sets a week per muscle suits most.
advice.detail: MEV≈10 → MAV≈20 sets/muscle (Renaissance Periodization landmarks).

advice.text: Start a training block at the lower end of your volume and add a set here and there as you adapt — leave yourself room to grow.
advice.detail: Progress MEV→MAV across a block (Renaissance Periodization).

advice.text: When progress stalls and everything feels heavy, take a planned easy week — clearing fatigue lets you push hard again.
advice.detail: Deloads manage the MRV/fatigue ceiling (Renaissance Periodization).

advice.text: Muscle is built in the day or two after training, not during the workout — which is why spreading sessions through the week pays off.
advice.detail: Protein synthesis roughly doubles by ~24 h, elevated ~24–48 h (Phillips 1997).

advice.text: Recovery is part of the program — the growth signal from a session runs about 24–48 hours, so give a muscle time before hammering it again.
advice.detail: MPS elevated ~24–48 h post-session (Phillips 1997).

advice.text: Pressed for time? You can keep the muscle you've built on as little as a third of your usual volume — even one hard set a week helps.
advice.detail: Size held on ~1/3 (older) to ~1/9 (young) of building volume over 32 wk (Bickel 2011).

advice.text: On a hectic week, don't skip entirely — a short, reduced session holds onto what you've built far better than nothing.
advice.detail: Minimal maintenance dose preserves gains (Bickel 2011).

advice.text: A week or two off won't cost you — strength holds for weeks. Rest when you need it; just don't let a layoff stretch into months.
advice.detail: Strength largely retained ~2–4 wk; longer breaks → atrophy (Mujika & Padilla 2000).

advice.text: Missed some sessions to illness or travel? A few won't erase months of work — just pick up where you left off.
advice.detail: Short cessation barely dents strength (Mujika & Padilla 2000).

advice.text: Chasing the "burn" or a big pump doesn't reliably mean more growth — total hard sets over the week is what counts.
advice.detail: Acute EMG/'activation' doesn't predict hypertrophy (Vigotsky 2022).

advice.text: How "activated" a muscle feels isn't a reliable guide to growth — track your weekly sets and whether the weights are climbing instead.
advice.detail: Feel/EMG is not a validated growth proxy (Vigotsky 2022).

advice.text: A consistent sleep schedule may matter more than how long you sleep — steady bed and wake times predicted lower death risk better than total hours.
advice.detail: Most-regular sleepers 20–48% lower all-cause mortality; regularity beat duration (Windred 2024, n=60,977).

advice.text: Around 1.6 g of protein per kg of bodyweight a day covers most lifters — much beyond that adds little.
advice.detail: Benefit plateaued ~1.6 g/kg/day; +~0.3 kg lean mass with protein (Morton 2018, meta-analysis of RCTs).

advice.text: Past ~1.6 g/kg, extra protein does little for muscle — your training and sleep move the needle more than another shake.
advice.detail: Diminishing returns above ~1.6 g/kg/day (Morton 2018).

advice.text: To build muscle, eat in a slight calorie surplus — trying to grow at maintenance is an uphill battle.
advice.detail: A modest surplus supports hypertrophy (Slater 2019, review).

advice.text: You can't build much muscle in a real calorie deficit — if size is the goal, eat at least at maintenance.
advice.detail: Energy availability constrains growth (Slater 2019).

advice.text: Gain slowly: about 0.25–0.5% of bodyweight a week keeps new gains mostly muscle, not fat.
advice.detail: Faster gain added more fat without more lean mass (Garthe 2013, RCT in elite athletes).

advice.text: Cutting? Keep protein high and keep lifting heavy — that's what tells your body to hold onto muscle while the fat comes off.
advice.detail: Resistance training + high protein preserves lean mass in a deficit (Helms 2014).

advice.text: On a cut, protein needs go up, not down — aim toward the higher end (~2 g/kg) to protect muscle in a deficit.
advice.detail: ~2.3–3.1 g/kg lean mass recommended during a deficit (Helms 2014).

advice.text: Losing fat? Keep it to about 0.5–1% of bodyweight a week — faster tends to drag your strength and muscle down with it.
advice.detail: ~0.5–1%/wk loss recommended to retain muscle (Helms 2014).

advice.text: Crash diets backfire — a moderate deficit you can actually sustain keeps more muscle and strength than slashing calories.
advice.detail: Aggressive deficits cost lean mass and performance (Helms 2014).

advice.text: Everyone chases protein and forgets fibre — yet ~25–30 g a day is one of the most consistent dietary links to a longer, healthier life.
advice.detail: +8 g/day ≈ 7% lower mortality; highest vs lowest 15–30% lower (Reynolds 2019, Lancet meta-analyses).

advice.text: Good news for coffee drinkers: 3–4 cups a day is linked to lower overall and heart-disease death risk — for most people it's more likely to help than harm.
advice.detail: 3–4 cups ≈ 17% lower all-cause mortality, RR 0.83 (Poole 2017, BMJ umbrella review). Not for everyone.

advice.text: Staying well-hydrated may help you age more slowly — adults whose blood sodium sat at the high end of normal showed faster aging and earlier illness.
advice.detail: Serum Na >142 ≈ +39% chronic disease, ~50% higher odds 'biologically older' (Dmitrieva 2023, ARIC, ~25 y).

advice.text: Most of the longevity benefit of walking shows up by about 7,000–8,000 steps a day — the famous 10,000 was a marketing slogan, not science.
advice.detail: Top vs bottom quartile ~40–53% lower mortality; plateau ~6–8k (60+) / 8–10k (<60) (Paluch 2022, meta-analysis of 15 cohorts).

advice.text: Short on time midweek? People who pack their exercise into just 1–2 days show about the same lower death risk as those who spread it out.
advice.detail: Weekend warriors ~30% lower all-cause mortality, HR 0.70 (O'Donovan 2017, n=63,591).

advice.text: Even with no formal workouts, three or four 1-minute bursts of huffing and puffing a day — stairs, a hard walk uphill — are linked to roughly a quarter to a third lower mortality.
advice.detail: Median 4.4 min/day VILPA ≈ 26–30% lower all-cause/cancer, 32–34% lower CVD mortality (Stamatakis 2022, Nat Med).

advice.text: Just 30–60 minutes of strength training a week is linked to the biggest drop in early-death risk — and piling on much more adds little for longevity.
advice.detail: ~30–60 min/wk ≈ 10–17% lower all-cause/CVD/cancer/diabetes mortality, J-shaped (Momma 2022, meta-analysis).

advice.text: There seems to be no "fit enough": across 122,000 people, higher cardio fitness kept tracking with lower death risk — and being unfit carried risk on par with smoking.
advice.detail: Elite vs low fitness HR 0.20 (low ~5× risk), no upper limit (Mandsager 2018, JAMA Netw Open).

advice.text: It's not just how long you sit, but how you sit — getting up to move for a couple of minutes every half hour is linked to lower risk, and steadier blood sugar.
advice.detail: Long unbroken bouts (≥60–90 min) tracked with higher mortality; short bouts (<30 min) fared best (Diaz 2017).

advice.text: Enjoy a sauna? In one long study, frequent sessions (4–7×/week) were linked to about half the heart-disease death risk of once a week — heat may stress the heart a little like exercise.
advice.detail: 4–7×/wk vs 1×: all-cause HR 0.60, CVD 0.50 (Laukkanen 2015) — single-sex cohort, weaker evidence.

advice.text: Grip strength tracks with lifespan better than blood pressure does — not because squeezing helps, but because it mirrors your whole-body strength and health.
advice.detail: Each 5 kg lower grip ≈ +16% all-cause, +17% CV mortality (Leong 2015, PURE, n=139,691).

advice.text: A quick self-check: can you lower to the floor and stand back up without using your hands? People who manage it easily in midlife tend to outlive those who can't.
advice.detail: Lowest scorers (0–3/10) ~5–6× the mortality of top scorers (Brito 2012, n=2,002) — single small cohort, weaker evidence.

advice.text: Feeling low or wired? Exercise is one of the most reliable mood-lifters — across hundreds of trials it eased depression and anxiety about as much as therapy or medication.
advice.detail: Physical activity vs usual care: depression −0.43, anxiety −0.42 effect size (Singh 2023, BJSM umbrella review of meta-analyses of RCTs).

advice.text: Aim for about two hours a week in nature — parks, woods or coast. People who hit ~120 minutes report better health and wellbeing; under that, the boost didn't show.
advice.detail: ≥120 min/week in nature linked to good health & high wellbeing; nothing below 120 min (White 2019, Sci Rep, n≈20,000).

advice.text: Doing kind things for others reliably nudges up your own happiness — the giver benefits too, whatever the act.
advice.detail: Acts of kindness → actor's wellbeing, small-to-medium effect d=0.28 (Curry 2018, meta-analysis, 27 studies).

advice.text: Small habits — noting what went well, savouring good moments, using your strengths — modestly but reliably lift wellbeing and ease low mood.
advice.detail: Positive-psychology interventions: wellbeing g=0.39, depression g=−0.39 (Carr 2021, meta-analysis, 347 studies).

advice.text: Weak social ties carry a mortality risk on the scale of smoking 15 cigarettes a day — so a regular catch-up with friends counts as real health behaviour.
advice.detail: Strong ties ≈ +50% odds of survival, OR 1.50 (Holt-Lunstad 2010, meta-analysis, 308,849 people).

advice.text: Optimists tend to live 11–15% longer and are far likelier to reach 85 — and outlook is something you can practise and shift.
advice.detail: Most vs least optimistic: 11–15% longer lifespan, 50–70% higher odds of reaching 85 (Lee 2019, PNAS).

advice.text: Having a clear sense of purpose — a reason to get up in the morning — is linked to lower mortality, regardless of wealth or health.
advice.detail: Lowest vs highest purpose ≈ 2.4× mortality, HR 2.43 (Alimujiang 2019, n=6,985).

advice.text: Book readers tend to live longer: around 30 minutes a day was linked to a 23% lower death risk over 12 years — and only books showed it, not newspapers.
advice.detail: Reading >3.5 h/wk: HR 0.80, ~23-month survival advantage (Bavishi 2016).

advice.text: Want to stay injury-free? Strength training is the best insurance there is — in a review of trials it cut injuries to under a third. (Stretching, despite its reputation, didn't help.)
advice.detail: Strength training RR ~0.32 (injuries <1/3), overuse RR 0.53; stretching no benefit (Lauersen 2014, meta-analysis of 25 RCTs, n=26,610).

advice.text: Prone to tweaky hamstrings? Adding Nordic curls (slow, controlled hamstring lowers) roughly halves the risk of pulling one.
advice.detail: Nordic hamstring exercise RR 0.49 (~51% fewer hamstring injuries) (van Dyk 2019, meta-analysis, 8,459 athletes).

advice.text: Sprained ankle? Modern guidelines say move it early rather than fully resting it — gentle weight-bearing plus balance work, once the sharp pain settles.
advice.detail: Early controlled mobilisation > immobilisation; balance training ~halves re-sprain risk (Vuurberg 2018, BJSM guideline). Not medical advice.

advice.text: Kneecap pain usually responds better to the right exercise than to rest — build hip and quad strength and manage how much you load it.
advice.detail: Combined hip+knee exercise therapy beats rest/passive care (Collins 2018, consensus). Not medical advice.

advice.text: For ordinary low-back pain, staying gently active beats bed rest — most episodes settle, and movement speeds recovery.
advice.detail: Guidelines: reassurance, stay active, avoid bed rest (Foster 2018, Lancet). Not medical advice.

advice.text: Niggly shoulder? Back off the painful overhead work and load it progressively — a corticosteroid shot adds little over good exercise.
advice.detail: Advice + home exercise matched full physio; injection no added benefit (Hopewell 2021, GRASP RCT, n=708). Not medical advice.

advice.text: Tennis/golfer's elbow loves loading, not rest — work it with manageable grip and gradual loading, and skip the steroid shot.
advice.detail: Steroid injection worsened 1-yr recurrence (72% vs 8% physio); load it instead (Coombes 2013 RCT / 2015 synthesis). Not medical advice.
